section_title: INTRODUCTION AND BLENDED PRESENTATION STYLE
content:
ICT welcomes viewers, announcing a blended presentation style for both his private mentorship group and
the YouTube mentorship community. This is a temporary measure due to time constraints after a holiday,
combining midweek reviews/commentary with YouTube video content.
The private mentorship group typically receives content on Wednesday, Monday, and Friday, while the
YouTube community receives videos on Tuesday and Thursday nights. This blended approach saves time
and will not be a regular occurrence.
ICT mentions being on holiday in Florida, doing 'gum shoe detective work,' and finding interesting things to
be discussed in coming weeks. Delays returning home and administrative tasks for his mentorship and
businesses led to this combined session.

section_title: DISTINCTION BETWEEN PRIVATE AND YOUTUBE MENTORSHIP
content:
The private mentorship group sees market analysis and calls moves before they happen, receiving a 'taste'
of this prior analysis. ICT will use a past Monday night analysis for the private group to transition into
teaching for the YouTube community.
He also teases future live streams, stating he will be doing 'everything they said I wouldn't do' in 2022.
For the private group, all videos need to be downloaded as the server will no longer be hosted after June 1,
2022. The private mentorship will transition to forum posts only, where ICT will type out commentary
directly on charts, assuming members are now 'charter members' and understand the references.

section_title: THE IMPORTANCE OF HOLISTIC UNDERSTANDING BEYOND ENTRY PATTERNS
content:
ICT plans to outline real-time intraday chart analysis during live streams, which will be quicker but will
involve his 'droning on about things' as price moves. He acknowledges that some listeners just want 'an
entry pattern' and 'a targeting technique,' but stresses that this is not sufficient for consistent success.
He states that if trading were that easy, 'everybody would be rich.' While acknowledging the 'growth period'
everyone goes through, he insists on teaching comprehensive insights rather than just isolated patterns.

section_title: YOUTUBE MODEL VS. PRIVATE MENTORSHIP DETAIL
content:
The private mentorship (charter members) can strip down what they've learned or make it as intricate and
complex as they desire. This deep level of detail can be 'overwhelming for the majority,' designed for 'freaks
that want to just know everything.'
In contrast, the YouTube model provides a 'stripped down to its chrome,' 'no fluff' approach, a 'bread and
butter' working model with 'very easy rules.' This model, in ICT's opinion, 'can make you a consistently
successful analyst,' though he cannot promise profitability.
His promise is to teach how to 'read the markets better than anybody else would be able to show you,' and
he consistently proves this through weekly outlines. The YouTube community sees results by focusing on
specific patterns at specific times.

section_title: PRICE SIGNATURES AND PRECISION IN TRADING
content:
Markets exhibit 'certain signatures in price' that repeat, such as:
- Creation of a fair value gap.
- Breaking a low.
- Price returning to fill the gap (partially, halfway, or completely).
ICT emphasizes that 'we don't need to be precise' or 'pinpoint precision accurate.' Even 'average' entries
can be profitable with 'sound money management,' leading to consistent setups. He reiterates his
commitment to teaching and increasing YouTube activity to counter detractors.

section_title: BULLISH BIAS
content:
ICT reviews his bullish dollar analysis for the private group. He had forecasted a move to the 100 level, then
101, and a second target of 101.947 (rounded to 103).
He states he is 'not calling a top here in dollar,' maintaining a long-term bullish stance. Any current
retracement is seen as a temporary pullback after hitting a 'premium level.'
His mind would only change about the dollar being bullish if price were to go 'below these lows in here,'
signaling a potential term high. Even then, it would take time to change his long-term bias. He allows for
'sloppy price action' and is 'not in a hurry to make decisions about actually going in and taking live trades
again yet' due to holiday volume (Passover) and money staying on the sidelines.

section_title: ALGORITHMIC PRICE DELIVERY
content:
Examining the hourly chart for the dollar index, ICT points out how price moved up to the 101 level, leaving
a 'clean' fair value gap. He highlights how 'the body's respecting that' (the fair value gap) is 'beautiful price
action' and 'algorithmic price action.'
He stresses that this is 'not buying and selling pressure,' 'not supply and demand,' 'not harmonic,' but the
'algorithm delivering price on its terms.' Speculators have the 'fortunate opportunity to be participants' but
will not be profitable using 'things we learned in books,' 'indicators,' or 'retail logic.'
He challenges new viewers who might consider this a 'conspiracy,' urging them to 'investigate the things I
suggest are happening in the marketplace' for a month to see if they hold true.

section_title: UNIVERSAL APPLICABILITY OF ICT CONCEPTS AND AVOIDING 'TEAM MENTALITY'
content:
ICT applies the YouTube model (broken low, fair value gap, equilibrium, 50 percent retracement) to the
hourly dollar chart, noting its fractal nature. He asserts that his concepts are applicable to 'any market'
forex, bonds, futures, and even crypto (though he doesn't trade crypto himself).
He warns against developing a 'team mentality' (e.g., 'futures traders love Nasdaq' or 'e-mini S&P'). He
clarifies he traded futures in the 90s. The principles he teaches are fundamental to how markets move and
are not specific to one asset class. He discourages blindly following others and encourages individual
investigation of his claims.

section_title: THE 'DOLLAR TOPPED' SENTIMENT
content:
ICT continues his dollar index analysis, highlighting a fair value gap and noting how the 'volume of the
move is inside the bodies of the candle.' He explains that the algorithm often refers back to imbalance
points, similar to a 'bookmark' in a book.
He describes how price dropped after hitting the 101 target, completing a 100-point (or pip) move above
the 100 level. He views the current price action as potentially creating a sentiment that 'the dollar has
topped,' but he is 'not convinced of that yet,' still anticipating higher prices after a retracement.

section_title: THE PERILS OF PICKING TOPS AND BOTTOMS
content:
ICT reiterates his long-term bullish dollar bias, stating that he doesn't mind being wrong or missing long
- term tops and bottoms. His focus is on institutional order flow, which is rooted in higher time frame bias.
He lectures on the danger of 'selective hearing,' where students only pay attention to 'buzzwords' like 'order
block,' 'fair value gap,' or 'break in market structure,' ignoring the broader context. He warns against making
sentiment ideas or trade justifications from single statements. Bullish dollar implies bearish foreign
currencies (Euro, British Pound, etc.). He stresses: 'Do not try to pick tops and bottoms. You don't need
that.'

section_title: DOLLAR INDEX 15-MINUTE: MANIPULATING SENTIMENT
content:
On the 15-minute chart, ICT points out 'sell side liquidity resting below these lows.' He notes that price
action is 'too clean' in building the idea that current levels are resistance, which he doesn't believe.
He postulates that if he were 'making the market for a dollar,' he would take it down below 100, perhaps to
99.95 or 99.92, to 'convince everybody out there that the dollar is probably topped' before moving it higher.
He empathizes with traders who experience this deceptive price action, stating 'I've been there folks, I'm
not making fun of you.'

section_title: ORDER BLOCK THEORY AND FUTURE DISCLOSURES
content:
ICT examines a 15-minute chart, identifying an hourly fair value gap and a subsequent imbalance. He then
points to two up-close candles as a 'bearish order block.' He explains an order block as where 'price was
delivered on the upside' right before 'displacement to the downside,' representing a 'change in the state of
delivery.'
He strongly refutes misconceptions about order blocks taught by others, calling them 'completely wrong'
and stemming from 'supply and demand' logic, not his theory. He reveals he has purposely withheld 'a lot of
things' about order block theory, even from his private mentorship, until his four books (one fiction, three
technical) are released. The second volume of his technical trilogy will 'divulge a lot about order blocks' to
establish his ownership and correct misinformation.

section_title: ALGORITHMIC LOGIC, STOP HUNTING, AND COUNTER-TREND TRADING
content:
ICT explains the algorithm uses 'reference points' (like an order block) but doesn't necessarily know
individual stops. However, it operates on the logic of common retail stop placement (e.g., 'put your stop
right above the high'). This predictable placement creates liquidity for the algorithm to target.
He clarifies that the order block signifies a 'change in the state of delivery,' moving lower after a pause. He
warns that trading counter to the 'higher time frame order flow' is risky and 'you can and will absolutely lose
money' doing it, especially for new traders trying to be 'nimble.' He advises new traders not to attempt
counter-trend trading, even though some experienced students successfully navigate these setups,
matching their 'contrarian' personality.

section_title: FOREX CHALLENGES AND PERSONAL
content:
RESPONSIBILITY
ICT reviews his successful forecast for the Euro-Dollar to reach a specific low, an example of higher time
frame bias. He explains why he is 'not trading forex at the moment':
- 'Sloppy' price action.
- 'Muddy' market conditions.
- Hindered trade and supply chain congestion, which reduce currency movement.
He reassures that forex is not 'broken' and the algorithm hasn't 'changed' because his teaching is based on
the 'internal engines' of price delivery. He emphasizes that teaching these concepts won't change the
market, as many traders 'cannot follow instructions' or 'rules' and 'want to fault somebody else' instead of
taking personal responsibility. He highlights that people are finding success with his free content, proving
its validity.

section_title: GROWTH, DISCIPLINE, AND MONEY MANAGEMENT
content:
ICT delves into the personal challenges of trading:
- Revealing uncomfortable truths about oneself (e.g., 'not as good as you thought you were').
- The necessity of overcoming resistance to personal growth.
- The importance of 'responsibility,' 'adherence to rules,' and 'enough time' (avoiding instant gratification).
He argues that true profitability comes from sound money management and adherence to rules, not
necessarily the 'system' itself. He suggests a 'coin flip' experiment in demo trading: heads buy, tails sell,
risking half percent, aiming for one percent, using a 30-pip stop. This demonstrates how difficult it is to blow
an account with proper risk management.
He then describes refining a 'trading algorithm' or 'recipe' by narrowing down:
- Number of trades.
- Days of the week to trade.
- Specific time of day to trade.
- Asset classes and specific markets.
- Bullish or bearish bias.
This process provides clear instructions for expected results.

section_title: MENTORSHIP PHILOSOPHY AND COMMUNITY SUPPORT
content:
ICT sees his role as pointing to where the market is likely to go, not forcing specific entry strategies, as
different personalities require different approaches. He acknowledges that not all students can master
every concept perfectly. He has seen both 'extremely good profitable traders' and 'failures' among his
students.
For new YouTube community members, he encourages a supportive environment, asking them 'Do not
gang up on them, don't make fun of them' when others express struggles. He emphasizes encouraging
those who are 'hurting' and 'frustrated' to 'keep going.' He shares his own experience of having to
persevere alone in the 90s.

section_title: NAVIGATING ASSET CLASSES AND LIVE STREAM EXPECTATIONS
content:
ICT's current strategy involves navigating to where markets are 'big movers,' as forex is currently 'a quiet
asset class,' 'asleep right now.' He expects forex to 'spring back to life' eventually. His live streams in May
will outline intraday 'draw on liquidity,' similar to how he forecasts daily lows/highs.
He explicitly warns viewers 'not to take that as an invitation to go and trade on that,' as it's meant for study
and 'tape reading.' He shares his personal experience of early 'dumb luck' in trading perpetual bull
markets, which tricked him into thinking he understood what he was doing. He wants to prevent others from
making the same mistake.
He clarifies that while futures are currently more active, he doesn't advocate a 'team futures' or 'team forex'
mentality. He states he started in futures in 1992 and can easily adapt if forex changes. Asset classes move
in and out of 'hot' periods, and experienced traders know how to navigate this.

section_title: CONTRARIAN TRADE EXAMPLE
content:
ICT analyzes the Euro-Dollar hourly chart, showing an aggressive drop below the daily low (1.0806).
During Passover week, price consolidated, then rallied, creating a fair value gap. He explains a contrarian
trade scenario:
- Daily low taken out (sell stops/liquidity cleared).
- Market now seeks buy side liquidity (likely to go higher).
- Energetic move off the daily low creates a fair value gap.
- Price retraces into the fair value gap, offering a buying opportunity.
- Target is the 62 percent retracement level or above the bodies of certain candles (optimal trade entry).
This 'bread and butter' setup would yield around 30-40 pips. However, he warns that it's 'not a style of trade
that I would recommend you as a new developing student because it's going against the order flow.'

section_title: EURO DOLLAR 15-MINUTE: COUNTER-TREND ENTRY LOGIC AND PATIENCE
content:
On the Euro-Dollar 15-minute chart, ICT highlights a 'clean' fair value gap created by 'displacement' after
trading back down to the old daily low. He explains the logic for a counter-trend move:
- Daily low was taken out (sell stops/liquidity).
- Market now seeks buy side liquidity (to go higher).
- Energetic move creates displacement and a fair value gap.
- Price retracing into this gap is a buying opportunity.
- Target is above short-term highs or the 62 percent retracement level.
He emphasizes that for an impatient or inexperienced trader, waiting for this retracement feels impossible.
He warns against rushing into trades based on incomplete understanding or incorrect biases, especially in
a market context where a longer-term directional bias might be different. He states that consistency 'takes
a little bit of time,' not days or months.

section_title: THE IMPORTANCE OF KNOWING WHEN NOT TO TRADE
content:
ICT quickly reviews the British Pound daily, hourly, and 15-minute charts. His assessment is unequivocal:
'There's nothing here. Absolutely zero, nothing in this chart. Nothing to trade on, not one thing.' He
describes the price action as 'sloppy,' 'messy,' and 'back and forth.'
He stresses the crucial lesson of 'knowing when not to do something.' When markets look like this, traders
should 'clos[e] the charts, turning your computer off and go do something you love to do.' He admits he
hasn't traded the British Pound in 'months' due to these messy conditions, which he attributes to global
trade issues, not algorithmic changes.
He reassures that 'salad days' (easy trading) for currencies will return, and patience during these dull
periods is key to avoiding burnout and maintaining discipline. This teaches traders to remove the 'fear of
missing out' (FOMO).

section_title: GAMBLER'S MENTALITY VS. DISCIPLINED TRADING
content:
ICT delivers a stern lecture on trading psychology and discipline:
- 'You do not need to trade every single day.'
- The goal is to understand 'why today or tomorrow is a day that's highly probable that your bias will deliver
to a target.' If you can't reasonably outline this, 'then you're gambling.'
- He does 'not promote gamblers,' 'gambling,' 'overleveraging,' or 'lucky over leverage trading.'
He promotes starting with 'one contract,' aiming to 'double your account' with the 'lowest leverage,'
emphasizing that 'money management does the heavy lifting.' He warns that 'rushing to make big money'
leads to blowing accounts. He describes the 'true test' for a student as reaching a point where they have
'no more money to put back in the account' and are 'questioning whether or not trading is for you.' This
state of having 'nothing to go in and gamble with' makes a student truly 'ready.'
He concludes that some people will never succeed due to lack of discipline, responsibility, and adherence
to rules, finding fault with 'everything but themselves,' which he equates to 'mental illness.'
CONCLUSION:

section_title: INDEPENDENT THINKING AND TIME & PRICE
content:
ICT emphasizes that the 'mental illness' of blaming others for trading failures is 'not permanent' and can be
corrected by 'responsible discipline,' a 'routine,' and 'being accountable to someone.' He fosters a
community of 'like-minded' individuals to empower 'independent thinking,' not a 'cult.' He encourages
students to 'go in, look at these markets with the lens I'm providing you' to verify his concepts.
He reiterates that 'consistent' trading does not mean 'it happens every single day in the same pair.' When
clear setups aren't present (like in the British Pound), 'you stand still.' This patience is 'hard' but crucial. He
shares his 30 years of experience, highlighting that books on 'system hopping' don't truly teach the lesson
until one blows accounts.
He concludes that sound trading is rooted in 'time and price,' not 'triangles and shapes and all these things
and moving averages and all this stuff,' or 'religious views' like diversions or harmonics. He urges traders to
ask basic questions:
- 'Where was it an hour ago?'
- 'Where was it last Friday?'
- 'Was it likely go today?'
- 'Where is it likely to go by the end of the week?'
